vulnerability.title: Add nodev Option to /dev/shm
vulnerability.description: The nodev mount option can be used to prevent creation of device files in /dev/shm. Legitimate character and block devices should not exist within temporary directories like /dev/shm. Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /dev/shm.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then function perform_remediation { mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" /dev/shm)" # If the mount point is not in /etc/fstab, get previous mount options from /etc/mtab if ! grep -q "$mount_point_match_regexp" /etc/fstab; then # runtime opts without some automatic kernel/userspace-added defaults previous_mount_opts=$(grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" /etc/mtab | head -1 | awk '{print $4}' \ | sed -E "s/(rw|defaults|seclabel|nodev)(,|$)//g;s/

triage_decision.reason: Adding the nodev mount option to /dev/shm is a standard STIG hardening step that prevents device files in a temporary filesystem. It involves only editing /etc/fstab or remounting the filesystem, with no impact on authentication, networking, or critical services. The change is low‑risk and can be rolled back easily.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystem permissions; potential temporary reboot for mount change

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
I’ll edit /etc/fstab to guarantee /dev/shm has the nodev option. First, locate the line that mounts /dev/shm (tmpfs). If it exists, I’ll modify the options column (fourth field) to include nodev, adding it only if it’s missing. If the line isn’t present, I’ll append a standard tmpfs entry with nodev. The edit will be done with sed -i, using a regex that matches a non‑commented line containing /dev/shm and inserts nodev into the options list without duplicating it. This ensures the nodev flag is present while preserving other existing options.

I realized the task required a sed solution, so I focused on editing `/etc/fstab`. The line for `/dev/shm` is a `tmpfs` entry with six fields. I need to add `nodev` to the options field only if it isn’t already present. I built a sed command that matches non‑comment lines containing `/dev/shm`, checks for the absence of `nodev`, and then inserts `nodev` after the existing options. The command is:

```bash
sed -i '/^[^#]*[[:space:]]\/dev\/shm[[:space:]]/ {
    /nodev/! s/\(\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)/\1 \2 \3,nodev \4/
}' /etc/fstab
```

This ensures the options field becomes `rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime` when `nodev` was missing, while leaving lines that already contain `nodev` unchanged.

I chose to modify `/etc/fstab` with `sed` because only `sed` is available.  
I target lines that mount `/dev/shm` and ensure they don’t already contain `nodev`.  
I capture the first four fields (device, mount point, type, options) and the fifth field (dump/pass).  
Then I replace the options field with itself plus `,nodev`, preserving the other fields.  
The final command:

```bash
sed -i '/^[^#]*[[:space:]]\/dev\/shm[[:space:]]/ { /nodev/! s/\(\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)\(\s\+\S\+\)/\1 \2 \3 \4,nodev \5/ }' /etc/fstab
```

This appends `nodev` to the options field for `/dev/shm` entries that lack it, without affecting variable field counts or existing `nodev` entries.

review_feedback: The sed command is fragile and not POSIX‑compatible, risking syntax errors or malformed /etc/fstab entries. No backup is created before in‑place editing, so a failure could corrupt the fstab and prevent proper mounting of /dev/shm, potentially causing boot issues or service disruptions. The plan also lacks a remount or verification step, so the change may not take effect immediately.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan targets the correct file and mount point and would add the nodev option, fixing the finding. However, the sed expression is fragile and may not work on all fstab formats, and the plan does not include a remount or verification step.
concerns: The sed command uses "\s" and unescaped parentheses which are not POSIX‑compatible and will likely cause a syntax error on default sed.
No backup of /etc/fstab is created before in‑place editing, risking loss of the original configuration if the command fails.
The plan does not remount /dev/shm or reboot, so the change may not take effect until later.
The regex assumes exactly four whitespace‑separated fields; extra spaces or tabs could break the substitution.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/fstab (e.g., cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak) before editing.

Use a more robust tool such as awk or sed -r with POSIX character classes, e.g., sed -i -r '/^[^#]*[[:space:]]+\/dev\/shm([[:space:]]|$)/{ /nodev/! s/^([^[:space:]]+)[[:space:]]+([^[:space:]]+)[[:space:]]+([^[:space:]]+)[[:space:]]+([^[:space:]]+)([[:space:]]+.*)$/\1 \2 \3 \4,nodev\5/ }' /etc/fstab
After editing, run "mount -o remount /dev/shm" (or reboot) to apply the new options immediately.
Verify the change by checking "findmnt -no OPTIONS /dev/shm" for the presence of nodev.
